plan: Sell all leveraged ETFs to eliminate that risk.
Trim semiconductor and broad index exposure significantly.
Reduce SPY concentration to meet mandate.
Add new positions in defensive healthcare and industrial sectors.
Focus on capital preservation and lower volatility.
conservative.read: This plan prioritizes capital preservation with lower volatility. Expect a modest target return around 1.5% per month. Significant reduction in leverage and concentration aims to minimize potential drawdowns.

plan: Reduce leveraged ETF exposure and trim semiconductor holdings.
Lower SPY concentration and reduce income ETF position.
Add new, high-scoring software and semiconductor names.
Balance broad index, tech, and some leverage.
Aim for risk-first growth with controlled drawdowns.
balanced.read: This balanced approach seeks growth while managing risk. Target returns are around 2.5% per month. It blends index exposure with quality tech and limited leverage, aiming for a steady climb with moderate drawdowns.

plan: Increase exposure to leveraged ETFs and semiconductors.
Add high-conviction software names from the scanner.
Reduce broad index concentration to meet mandate.
Eliminate income ETF to free up capital for growth.
Aggressively pursue higher returns with increased risk.
aggressive.read: This aggressive plan targets rapid growth, aiming for ~4% per month. It leans heavily into leveraged ETFs and top-rated tech names, accepting higher potential drawdowns. Daily rebalancing of leveraged ETFs can impact performance, and strict stop discipline is crucial to manage significant risk.

plan: Sell all leveraged and high-beta technology ETFs.
Reduce semiconductor and broad index exposure.
Significantly increase allocation to income-focused ETF.
Prioritize steady monthly distributions over growth.
Focus on capital preservation and low, reliable income.
income.read: This income-focused plan prioritizes stable monthly distributions, targeting ~1% per month. It shifts away from growth and volatility towards income generation. While aiming for low drawdowns, the Net Asset Value can still decline in market selloffs.
